title: Backlog Age and Breach Risk Forecast
dataSources: Primary open-queue posture from index=itsm sourcetype snow:incident (sys_id, number, opened_at, priority, assignment_group, state, incident_state, major_incident_state, parent_incident, sys_updated_on) replicated by Splunk Add-on for ServiceNow Table API polling; complement with sourcetype snow:case or snow:sn_customerservice_case when leadership tracks case backlog parity, plus sourcetype snow:sc_request for fulfillment queues sharing the same hour-target ladder. Engine fidelity layer: index=itsm sourcetype snow:task_sla carrying task reference sys_id, percentage, business_percentage, stage, business_elapsed_time, planned_end_time so Splunk pct_of_sla can mirror ServiceNow SLA engine math instead of pure calendar deltas alone.
app: Splunk_TA_snow — Splunk Add-on for ServiceNow ([Splunkbase 1928](https://splunkbase.splunk.com/app/1928)) on Heavy Forwarder modular servicenow_record_input collectors plus Search Head parsing apps under $SPLUNK_HOME/etc/apps/Splunk_TA_snow.
spl:
index=itsm (sourcetype="snow:incident" OR sourcetype="snow:case" OR sourcetype="snow:sc_request") earliest=-24h@h latest=now
| eval _time=strptime(sys_updated_on, "%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S")
| dedup sys_id sortby -_time
| eval st=coalesce(state, incident_state, "")
| where st!="Closed" AND st!="Resolved" AND st!="Cancelled" AND NOT (tonumber(st)==6 OR tonumber(st)==7)
| where isnull(major_incident_state) OR major_incident_state=""
| eval opened_epoch=strptime(opened_at, "%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S")
| eval age_hours=(now()-opened_epoch)/3600
| eval wday=strftime(now(), "%A")
| eval wkend_off=case(match(lower(wday),"^(saturday|sunday)$"), min(48, age_hours*0.35), true(), 0)
| eval bh_age_hours=case(match(lower(wday),"^(saturday|sunday)$"), max(0.01, age_hours-wkend_off), true(), age_hours)
| eval priority_n=tonumber(priority)
| eval sla_target_hours=case(priority_n==1 OR priority=="1",4, priority_n==2 OR priority=="2",8, priority_n==3 OR priority=="3",24, priority_n==4 OR priority=="4",72, true(),168)
| join type=left max=0 sys_id [ search index=itsm sourcetype="snow:task_sla" earliest=-7d@d | stats latest(percentage) as tsla_pct by task | rename task as sys_id ]
| eval pct_of_sla=if(isnotnull(tsla_pct) AND tsla_pct>0, round(tsla_pct,0), round(100*bh_age_hours/sla_target_hours,0))
| eval breach_risk=case(pct_of_sla>=100,"BREACHED", pct_of_sla>=90,"IMMINENT", pct_of_sla>=80,"AT_RISK", pct_of_sla>=60,"WATCH", true(),"OK")
| eval sla_remaining_hours=round(sla_target_hours-bh_age_hours,3)
| join type=left assignment_group priority_n [ search index=itsm sourcetype="snow:incident" earliest=-30d@d latest=@h | eval _time=strptime(sys_updated_on, "%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S") | dedup sys_id sortby -_time | eval st2=coalesce(state, incident_state) | eval sn=tonumber(st2) | eval sl=lower(trim(tostring(st2))) | where sn IN (6,7) OR sl IN ("resolved","closed") | eval op=strptime(opened_at, "%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S") | eval cl=strptime(coalesce(closed_at, resolved_at), "%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S") | eval hist_h=(cl-op)/3600 | eval priority_n=tonumber(priority) | where hist_h>0 AND hist_h<720 | stats avg(hist_h) as historical_close_avg by assignment_group priority_n ]
| eval historical_close_avg=round(coalesce(historical_close_avg, sla_target_hours*0.45),2)
| eval forecasted_breach_eta=round((sla_target_hours-bh_age_hours)-historical_close_avg,2)
| eval at_risk=if(pct_of_sla>=80 AND historical_close_avg>sla_remaining_hours,1,0)
| where breach_risk!="OK"
| eval breach_sort=case(breach_risk=="BREACHED",50,breach_risk=="IMMINENT",40,breach_risk=="AT_RISK",30,breach_risk=="WATCH",20,1,10)
| sort - breach_sort - pct_of_sla
| table number, assignment_group, priority, bh_age_hours, sla_target_hours, pct_of_sla, breach_risk, historical_close_avg, sla_remaining_hours, forecasted_breach_eta, at_risk
description: Forward-looking operations search that ranks still-open ServiceNow work by percent-of-target elapsed time, prefers replicated task_sla percentages when Splunk carries them, folds a coarse weekend adjustment onto raw opened-to-now hours, blends thirty-day historic closure pace per resolver group and priority, and emits breach outlook bands plus ETA math filtered to rows that already left the OK lane so capacity leads intervene before weekly breach reporting catches up.
value: Resolver directors gain an actionable shortlist sorted by impending breach severity rather than oldest-first anecdotes: recruiting overtime or borrowing analysts when forecasted breach_eta turns negative beats discovering overload from executive SLA breach decks days later. Matching Splunk forecasts with assignment_group-aware staffing lookups ties dollars and contractor surge approvals to projected backlog absorption measured in hours, not vibes—especially when sibling retrospective SLA dashboards lag twenty-four hours behind poll-driven snapshots.
implementation: (1) Confirm Splunk Add-on for ServiceNow (1928) poll intervals cover incident plus task_sla within fifteen minutes for backlog freshness; (2) publish assignment_group→capacity_team routing lookup on Search Heads; (3) schedule the breach outlook alert every fifteen minutes with lookup-throttled routing; (4) pair Dashboard Studio backlog tiles with UC-23.7.1 retrospective SLA tiles so executives see predictive posture beside realized breaches.
visualization: (1) Dashboard Studio splunk.table sorted by breach_sort mirroring saved search columns with colour rules on breach_risk and at_risk. (2) splunk.singlevalue pairings for count of AT_RISK plus IMMINENT rows distinct from watch totals. (3) Trellis splunk.bar by assignment_group showing backlog counts stacked by breach_risk using companion search without terminal breach filter. (4) Drilldown hyperlink template opening ServiceNow incident.do via sys_id token passthrough.

references.title: ServiceNow Docs — SLA engine overview (task SLA concepts)
Splunk Docs — Splunk Add-on for ServiceNow troubleshooting
ServiceNow Docs — REST Table API reference

knownFalsePositives: Weekend-and-holiday drift: Splunk wall-clock pct_of_sla can look crisis-level while ServiceNow task_sla.business_time_left still shows comfortable room because nights and bank holidays freeze the engine clock your raw age math ignores—reconcile with snow:task_sla before reassigning squads Friday evening. On-hold pause blind spot: awaiting-caller vendor hold states stop the contractual clock in Snow but Splunk bh_age_hours keeps climbing, so breach_risk reads AT_RISK while the related list shows stage paused—add hold reason fields or child wait tables when triage escalations depend on fairness. Same ticket reopened after “fix” loops: multiple resolve cycles reset task_sla rows; Splunk opened_at spans the full multi-month story so forecasted breach_eta overstates urgency relative to the active clock—exclude chronic accounts or parent splitters per policy. Handoff-heavy groups: backlog age tied to current assignment_group misallocates blame when ninety percent of elapsed time sat with a predecessor queue—pair with reassignment histograms before capacity funding moves. Planned long-run P4 roadmap items parked for two quarters rarely breach even with high wall hours—filter category u_type for enhancement versus break-fix so the watch list does not shame product planning tickets. Release weekend surges that clear in forty-eight hours spike row volume without sustained breach velocity—alert on consecutive poll increases, not single batch imports. Parent incident with unfinished child tasks: closing subtasks does not retire the parent sys_id Splunk ages; breach_risk stays red on the umbrella while children look green unless you model parent filters for MIM exclusions already in place.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Splunk Enterprise 9.2+ or Splunk Cloud; analyst role with srchIndexesAllowed covering the ITSM index used below (rename index=itsm everywhere if your Splunk Add-on for ServiceNow lands on index=business).
• Splunk Add-on for ServiceNow (Splunk_TA_snow, Splunkbase 1928) on a Heavy Forwarder with OAuth client credentials for Table API polling, plus props/transforms coverage on Search Heads for sourcetype snow:incident, snow:task_sla, and optional snow:case / snow:sc_request when those tables are enabled.
• ServiceNow integration user rights to read incident (and optional case / sc_request), plus task_sla for percentage-of-SLA replication; confirm Performance Analytics business calendars in UI when validating business-hour divergence called out in Step 5.
• Capacity-planning charter: ops leadership agrees priority-to-hour targets (example mapping P1=4h / P2=8h / P3=24h / P4=72h wall-clock placeholders) match internal policy—the SPL holds those constants until you promote lookup-driven targets.

Step 1 — Configure data collection
Enable modular inputs or inputs.conf servicenow_record_input stanzas that poll sys_updated_on checkpointed snapshots into index=itsm with sourcetype snow:incident (canonical Table incident), extending the field projection list to opened_at, closed_at, resolved_at, priority, assignment_group, major_incident_state, parent_incident, state, incident_state, sys_id, number, sys_updated_on. Poll interval 60–300 seconds for near-real-time backlog posture; throttle Table API pagination if OAuth tokens refresh every thirty minutes.
Add a dedicated task_sla input toward the same index with sourcetype snow:task_sla, requesting at minimum task, percentage, business_percentage, stage, business_elapsed_time, has_breached, sla, planned_end_time so Splunk can prefer engine-native pct_of_sla when replicated. Optional case and request tables use sourcetype labels snow:case and snow:sc_request in many deployments—normalize field aliases in props if opened_at differs (opened_at versus opened_date).
Document timezone truth: ServiceNow stores GMT-friendly timestamps; Splunk derives _time from sys_updated_on on poll snapshots while age_hours uses now() minus opened_epoch in UTC unless you wrap strptime with zone-aware evaluation for display panels only.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Save the SPL from the spl field as a scheduled alert every fifteen to thirty minutes over earliest=-24h@h latest=now so each run refreshes deduped open tickets before breaches surface in executive SLA dashboards. Trigger when result count rises above zero after the closing breach_risk!="OK" filter, route to assignment_group DL mapped through a Splunk lookup (capacity_team_routing.csv), throttle four hours per assignment_group plus priority tuple to prevent paging storms during ingest stalls.
Clone a companion scheduled search without the terminal breach_risk filter for backlog dashboards that still show OK rows when managers request green/yellow mix charts.

```spl
index=itsm (sourcetype="snow:incident" OR sourcetype="snow:case" OR sourcetype="snow:sc_request") earliest=-24h@h latest=now
| eval _time=strptime(sys_updated_on, "%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S")
| dedup sys_id sortby -_time
| eval st=coalesce(state, incident_state, "")
| where st!="Closed" AND st!="Resolved" AND st!="Cancelled" AND NOT (tonumber(st)==6 OR tonumber(st)==7)
| where isnull(major_incident_state) OR major_incident_state=""
| eval opened_epoch=strptime(opened_at, "%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S")
| eval age_hours=(now()-opened_epoch)/3600
| eval wday=strftime(now(), "%A")
| eval wkend_off=case(match(lower(wday),"^(saturday|sunday)$"), min(48, age_hours*0.35), true(), 0)
| eval bh_age_hours=case(match(lower(wday),"^(saturday|sunday)$"), max(0.01, age_hours-wkend_off), true(), age_hours)
| eval priority_n=tonumber(priority)
| eval sla_target_hours=case(priority_n==1 OR priority=="1",4, priority_n==2 OR priority=="2",8, priority_n==3 OR priority=="3",24, priority_n==4 OR priority=="4",72, true(),168)
| join type=left max=0 sys_id [ search index=itsm sourcetype="snow:task_sla" earliest=-7d@d | stats latest(percentage) as tsla_pct by task | rename task as sys_id ]
| eval pct_of_sla=if(isnotnull(tsla_pct) AND tsla_pct>0, round(tsla_pct,0), round(100*bh_age_hours/sla_target_hours,0))
| eval breach_risk=case(pct_of_sla>=100,"BREACHED", pct_of_sla>=90,"IMMINENT", pct_of_sla>=80,"AT_RISK", pct_of_sla>=60,"WATCH", true(),"OK")
| eval sla_remaining_hours=round(sla_target_hours-bh_age_hours,3)
| join type=left assignment_group priority_n [ search index=itsm sourcetype="snow:incident" earliest=-30d@d latest=@h | eval _time=strptime(sys_updated_on, "%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S") | dedup sys_id sortby -_time | eval st2=coalesce(state, incident_state) | eval sn=tonumber(st2) | eval sl=lower(trim(tostring(st2))) | where sn IN (6,7) OR sl IN ("resolved","closed") | eval op=strptime(opened_at, "%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S") | eval cl=strptime(coalesce(closed_at, resolved_at), "%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S") | eval hist_h=(cl-op)/3600 | eval priority_n=tonumber(priority) | where hist_h>0 AND hist_h<720 | stats avg(hist_h) as historical_close_avg by assignment_group priority_n ]
| eval historical_close_avg=round(coalesce(historical_close_avg, sla_target_hours*0.45),2)
| eval forecasted_breach_eta=round((sla_target_hours-bh_age_hours)-historical_close_avg,2)
| eval at_risk=if(pct_of_sla>=80 AND historical_close_avg>sla_remaining_hours,1,0)
| where breach_risk!="OK"
| eval breach_sort=case(breach_risk=="BREACHED",50,breach_risk=="IMMINENT",40,breach_risk=="AT_RISK",30,breach_risk=="WATCH",20,1,10)
| sort - breach_sort - pct_of_sla
| table number, assignment_group, priority, bh_age_hours, sla_target_hours, pct_of_sla, breach_risk, historical_close_avg, sla_remaining_hours, forecasted_breach_eta, at_risk
```

Step 3 — Pipeline mechanics
The opening command binds index=itsm to incident-family sourcetypes with explicit earliest/latest windows so backlog posture reflects recently polled snapshots rather than historical closures. dedup sys_id sortby -_time collapses duplicate polls into the newest lifecycle image before eligibility filters run. Two-stage state filtering removes Closed, Resolved, and Cancelled representations plus numeric closures while optionally stripping active Major Incident rows from routine capacity math when major_incident_state carries the war-room flag your MIM process sets.
opened_epoch converts opened_at text into epoch seconds; age_hours divides the gap between now() and opened_epoch by 3600 per requirements. Weekend crude adjustment subtracts wkend_off from Saturday and Sunday rows so a Saturday queue review does not treat the whole two days as pure elapsed SLA consumption when your ServiceNow calendars already pause clocks—this is an approximate shadow, not a replica of cmn_schedule.
priority_n and sla_target_hours map numeric or string priority to four hour bands. The first join reattaches replicated task_sla.percentage when present so pct_of_sla matches the ServiceNow engine when Splunk carries the live engine value; otherwise Splunk derives percent from adjusted hours divided by sla_target_hours. breach_risk buckets into BREACHED, IMMINENT, AT_RISK, WATCH, or OK using the 100/90/80/60 ladder. The second join computes historical_close_avg from thirty days of resolved tickets grouped by assignment_group and priority_n so forward projection compares remaining allowance to recent team velocity. forecasted_breach_eta subtracts historical average close duration from remaining hours; at_risk lights when pct_of_sla already passed eighty percent yet historical_close_avg exceeds sla_remaining_hours. Non-OK rows surface for routing, sorted by bespoke breach_sort then pct_of_sla.

Step 4 — Validate
Pick five Splunk rows covering each breach_risk band when available. Confirm against the ServiceNow incident UI that state, priority, assignment_group, opened_at, and Major Incident toggles match Splunk. Open the Task SLA related list for the same sys_id and read percentage, business_percentage, stage, and business time left—when task_sla replication is healthy Splunk pct_of_sla should land within a few points unless weekends or holidays diverge as noted in Step 5.
Spot-check the thirty-day historical_close_avg join by exporting closed incidents from ServiceNow Performance Analytics or a list report filtered to the same assignment_group and priority band—manual averages should approximate Splunk within variance driven by reopen exclusions or filtering caps on hist_h.

Step 5 — Operational caveats and known false positives
• Calendar versus business-hours divergence: Splunk now() stays wall-clock UTC while ServiceNow task_sla stops clocks during nights, weekends, and holidays defined in cmn_schedule and business_calendar. Without replicated percentage fields, Splunk pct_of_sla can read thirty to sixty points higher than ServiceNow around weekends—treat Splunk-derived percentages as directional unless snow:task_sla joins stay fresh within two polls.
• Pause and resume while On Hold or awaiting vendor: ServiceNow pauses SLA timers when wait states flip true, yet Splunk bh_age_hours still grows from opened_at unless you subtract paused durations via supplemental joins or pause flags—queues look hotter than contractual clocks imply for vendor-dependent tickets.
• Intake mis-label priority: Tickets logged as P3 while behaving like major incidents balloon forecasted breach_eta because sla_target_hours shrinks versus reality—cross-check assignment_group swaps from UC intake retrospectives monthly.
• Assignment_group churn mid-flight: Aggregating backlog by current group hides age accrued before handoff; forecast accuracy drops until historical_close_avg inherits enough volume after reroutes—consider trending sys_audit reassignment deltas separately when churn spikes.
• Campaign or release surge noise: Burst arrivals inflate assignment_group backlog counts while median closure stays fast—capacity spikes without proportional breach risk until sustained pct_of_sla climbs across consecutive polls.
• CAB or approval gates: Tickets pending change advisory approval remain technically In Progress while blocked—Splunk treats elapsed hours as capacity strain though SLA clocks often pause waiting approvals—annotate CAB-heavy categories before blaming resolver throughput alone.

When Splunk pct_of_sla diverges sharply from ServiceNow with empty tsla_pct values on every row, investigate missing fields on replicated snapshots before escalating staffing—OAuth HTTP 401 bursts on the Heavy Forwarder mirror Table API outages described in Splunk Add-on runbooks.
grandmaExplanation: We look at open tickets and estimate which ones will cross the promised finish window next, using how fast each team usually closes similar work. That way managers can move people onto the hottest queues before customers feel ignored or weekly leadership decks show another breach spike.
